description: Repo baseline stays green before Voice Bridge work begins so new harness sessions can resume from a verified `init.sh` pass instead of inheriting unrelated breakage.
steps: Run `bash init.sh` from the worktree and confirm the repo baseline passes before Voice Bridge implementation starts.
Verify brittle tests touched during bootstrap remain green under `.venv/bin/pytest` so later harness rounds can trust the baseline.
Record the baseline verification outcome in `PROGRESS.md` before marking the feature complete.

description: A new harness task is initialized for Voice Bridge v1 with a granular PLAN and append-only progress log so future sessions can resume without re-deriving scope.
steps: Create `PLAN.json` with the Voice Bridge v1 feature list and leave every `passes` field false on first write.
Create `PROGRESS.md` with the initial task summary, baseline state, and current blockers.
Commit the initialization checkpoint before beginning the first implementation feature.

description: The `.harness/` workspace exists for planner/generator/evaluator communication and follows the harness-engineering file protocol.
steps: Create `.harness/` inside the worktree and keep the expected planner/build/QA/status file layout.
Update `.gitignore` so `.harness/` is treated as harness communication state rather than product source.
Verify the local harness files can be recreated or inspected without polluting normal repo status.

description: The approved Voice Bridge v1 product spec is persisted in `.harness/spec.md` so later generator and evaluator rounds work from the same architecture and acceptance rules.
steps: Write `.harness/spec.md` with the approved three-layer Voice Bridge architecture, v1 scope, and future ingress/backend reservation.
Ensure the spec explicitly treats `iPhone Siri` as the only v1 runtime success target and `HomePod` as a future experiment.
Verify the spec documents the self-contained subtree requirement for later repo extraction.

description: Round-one build scope is formalized in `.harness/contract.md` so the first generator pass has concrete success criteria instead of open-ended implementation drift.
steps: Write `.harness/contract.md` for round one with a bounded feature slice tied back to the spec.
Include user-visible and testable success criteria for each round-one capability.
Verify the contract names explicit validation commands or checks rather than vague completion claims.

description: The current Apple toolchain gate is recorded explicitly so the harness does not silently downgrade iOS validation when full Xcode or iOS SDK support is missing.
steps: Run `xcode-select -p` and record the active developer directory in progress and harness status.
Run `xcodebuild -showsdks` and `xcrun simctl list devices available` to confirm whether full Xcode and simulator tooling exist.
Persist the missing-toolchain result in `.harness/status.json` and `PROGRESS.md` if the machine is still limited to Command Line Tools.

description: A self-contained `ios/VoiceBridge/` root is created so all iOS-specific code can later move into a dedicated repository without pulling nanobot runtime internals along.
steps: Create `ios/VoiceBridge/` as the top-level subtree for the new app and bridge code.
Keep app shell, core, docs, and tests under that subtree instead of scattering files across the repo root.
Verify nothing inside the subtree requires relative imports from the Python codebase.

description: The iOS subtree is split into `AppShell`, `BridgeCore`, and `Docs` so future repo extraction has clean ownership boundaries from day one.
steps: Create directories or targets that correspond to `AppShell`, `BridgeCore`, and `Docs` responsibilities.
Document each boundary so product UI, bridge logic, and operational docs do not collapse into one module.
Verify the file layout matches the three-part split described in the spec.

description: The iOS subtree includes a migration note that explains how to move Voice Bridge into a future standalone repository without changing runtime behavior.
steps: Add an `ios/VoiceBridge/README.md` or equivalent entrypoint that describes the subtree layout and migration intent.
Document which files can move as a unit and which runtime contract with nanobot must stay stable.
Verify the migration note does not assume the presence of Python package internals.

description: User-facing branding stays localizable while internal code names remain platform-neutral so the bridge can later target nanobot, openclaw, or other assistants without a rewrite.
steps: Centralize user-visible assistant naming so the Siri phrase can say `纳博特` without hard-coding nanobot-specific labels everywhere.
Keep internal types and folders named around `VoiceBridge` rather than a single backend brand.
Verify future backend switching would not require renaming the core data models.

description: A `BackendKind` abstraction exists so the bridge can start with nanobot while reserving future backend routing for openclaw and other services.
steps: Add a bridge-core enum or equivalent model for backend selection.
Include `nanobot` as the only active v1 runtime case and reserve `openclaw` as a future-facing placeholder.
Verify the rest of BridgeCore consumes the abstraction rather than hard-coding nanobot everywhere.

description: A `SourcePlatform` abstraction captures where a request came from so Siri/HomePod/小爱/天猫/车机 expansion can reuse the same internal protocol later.
steps: Add a platform model with at least `siri`, `homepod`, `xiaoai`, `tmallgenie`, `carplay`, and `custom` cases.
Default v1 runtime wiring to `siri` while leaving the others reserved and documented.
Verify the model is used by `BridgeRequest` instead of leaving source metadata implicit.

description: A `SourceDeviceType` abstraction records whether the caller is a phone, speaker, car head unit, or another surface without leaking platform-specific logic into backend code.
steps: Add a device-type model such as `phone`, `speaker`, `carHeadUnit`, and `custom`.
Set the iPhone Siri path to the phone device type in v1.
Verify backend requests only see the normalized device metadata rather than App Intent specifics.

description: BridgeCore owns a first-class `BridgeRequest` model so all ingress adapters normalize prompt metadata the same way before routing to a backend.
steps: Implement `BridgeRequest` with backend, speaker, sessionId, prompt, sourcePlatform, and sourceDeviceType fields.
Ensure the model treats the prompt text as the canonical payload rather than raw audio or Siri-specific state.
Verify the request model is serializable or otherwise testable independent of the app shell.

description: BridgeCore owns a first-class `BridgeResponse` model that separates backend truth from how the client displays or speaks the answer.
steps: Implement `BridgeResponse` with at least reply, endConversation, displayText, and spokenText.
Make the raw backend `reply` the canonical value and derive display or spoken variants from it.
Verify the response model can represent both short and truncated long replies.

description: A `BridgeConfig` model exists so backend selection, base URL, and credentials are explicit runtime inputs rather than hidden app globals.
steps: Add a configuration model with backend kind, base URL, and API key fields.
Keep the config representation independent of SwiftUI screens so it can move into a standalone core package later.
Verify missing or invalid config can be detected before a network request is sent.

description: BridgeCore exposes a backend protocol that ingress adapters call instead of constructing HTTP requests directly inside App Intents or views.
steps: Define a protocol such as `BridgeBackend` with an async send method returning `BridgeResponse`.
Keep the protocol signature centered on `BridgeRequest` and `BridgeResponse`.
Verify app shell and Siri intent code depend on the protocol rather than a concrete nanobot client.

description: A concrete `NanobotBackend` bridges Voice Bridge requests into nanobot's `/chat` API without leaking nanobot-specific transport code throughout the app.
steps: Implement a nanobot backend client that consumes `BridgeConfig` and `BridgeRequest`.
Map the request into `POST /chat` using the expected headers and body shape.
Verify the backend returns a normalized `BridgeResponse` rather than raw JSON.

description: An `OpenClawBackend` placeholder or reserved extension point exists so future backend work does not require reshaping the bridge core types.
steps: Reserve an openclaw backend case or placeholder type without implementing runtime network behavior in v1.
Document that v1 intentionally leaves openclaw unimplemented while preserving the interface slot.
Verify unsupported future backends fail clearly rather than silently defaulting to nanobot.

description: The nanobot backend encodes `/chat` requests in the agreed body format so Siri prompts reach nanobot with stable field names.
steps: Encode request bodies as `{"text":"<prompt>","speaker":"siri-iphone"}` for the v1 Siri path.
Keep any additional metadata out of the nanobot payload unless it is explicitly supported.
Verify encoding tests cover the JSON field names and speaker value.

description: The nanobot backend decodes `/chat` responses into bridge responses so Siri playback logic never has to parse raw transport JSON itself.
steps: Decode `reply` and `end_conversation` from the backend response.
Map those decoded fields into `BridgeResponse` with sensible display and spoken defaults.
Verify response decoding tests cover both standard and optional-field cases.

description: Bearer authentication is attached to bridge requests so the app can call secured nanobot instances without hard-coding credentials in source.
steps: Attach `Authorization: Bearer <apiKey>` when a configured API key is available.
Reject missing required credentials before sending a request when the config is incomplete.
Verify request-construction tests assert the authorization header behavior.

description: Timeout behavior is normalized into user-facing bridge errors so Siri can speak an actionable failure instead of ending with a generic system acknowledgement.
steps: Map request timeouts to a stable bridge error describing that nanobot did not respond in time.
Ensure the timeout error can be surfaced both in the app UI and Siri dialog text.
Verify the timeout mapping with a focused unit test.

description: Authentication failures are normalized into bridge errors so a bad or missing API key produces a clear spoken remediation hint.
steps: Map HTTP 401 or equivalent auth failures to a dedicated bridge error.
Return a user-facing message that instructs the operator to configure the server or API key in the app.
Verify the auth error mapping with a focused unit test.

description: Network, transport, and invalid-JSON failures are normalized into bridge errors so Siri does not collapse them into a misleading `完成` response.
steps: Map offline, connection, and invalid response payload failures to dedicated bridge errors.
Ensure these errors carry user-facing text suitable for Siri playback and on-screen display.
Verify the mapping with focused tests for offline and malformed JSON cases.

description: The v1 Siri bridge uses a stable speaker value so downstream nanobot logs and policies can distinguish iPhone Siri traffic from HomePod or future adapters.
steps: Define the v1 speaker strategy so App Intent requests use `siri-iphone` when building the backend payload.
Keep the mapping in bridge core rather than scattering string literals through views and intents.
Verify the speaker value appears in encoded `/chat` requests.

description: A session-id strategy exists so future multi-turn or multi-device routing can correlate requests without redesigning the protocol surface.
steps: Add session-id generation or transport to `BridgeRequest` even if v1 uses a simple ephemeral value.
Keep the session identifier creation separate from the backend request encoder.
Verify repeated manual requests create or carry session ids consistently.

description: Reply formatting distinguishes between display text and spoken text so long answers remain useful in the app without making Siri read excessive content.
steps: Implement a reply-formatting policy that preserves full display text while optionally truncating spoken text.
Use a clear appendage such as `完整回复已保存在纳博特应用中` for spoken truncation cases.
Verify the truncation behavior with focused unit tests.

description: A local history entry model exists so the bridge can keep a recent record of prompts and replies for debugging without introducing backend persistence coupling.
steps: Define a history item model containing at least timestamp, prompt, reply, and backend metadata.
Keep the model inside the self-contained iOS subtree rather than reusing Python-side message classes.
Verify the model can represent both successful replies and surfaced errors.

description: A recent-history store keeps the latest 20 interactions locally so Siri or network failures can be diagnosed without leaving the app.
steps: Implement a local history store that appends interactions and trims older entries beyond the cap.
Expose the stored entries to app-shell screens without coupling them to the networking layer.
Verify the store retains only the most recent 20 entries.

description: A configuration store persists the selected backend, base URL, and API key locally so the operator does not need to re-enter settings before each Siri test.
steps: Persist bridge configuration locally on-device using a simple store such as `UserDefaults` for v1.
Keep persistence behind a small abstraction so future extraction or security upgrades remain localized.
Verify changed settings are loaded back into the app on the next launch or state refresh.

description: An app-level state container coordinates config, manual testing, and recent history so views and intents share one bridge runtime model.
steps: Add an app-shell model that owns current config, request execution, and recent history updates.
Inject the model into SwiftUI screens and Siri intent handling through a stable boundary.
Verify manual requests update both the latest reply and recent-history state.

description: A minimal app entrypoint exists so the Voice Bridge app can be opened, configured, and used for non-Siri smoke tests on the device.
steps: Add the main SwiftUI app entry for the Voice Bridge shell.
Ensure the entrypoint boots the shared state container and routes to the configuration/testing surfaces.
Verify the app can present the primary screens without Siri involvement.

description: A settings screen captures the backend URL and API key so operators can configure the bridge without editing source code or plist files.
steps: Add a settings view with editable base URL and API key fields.
Validate obvious configuration mistakes before saving or sending requests.
Verify updated settings persist and are reused by manual and Siri-triggered requests.

description: A manual test screen sends arbitrary prompts to the configured backend so the bridge can be debugged without relying on Siri every time.
steps: Add a manual test view with a prompt input and send action.
Show the most recent reply or surfaced error after each manual request.
Verify sending `你好` through the manual screen produces the same backend behavior as a direct `/chat` smoke test.

description: A history screen exposes recent prompts and replies so operators can inspect what Siri or manual tests actually sent and received.
steps: Add a recent-history screen backed by the local history store.
Display prompt, reply, and time metadata clearly enough for debugging.
Verify new interactions appear in history after manual or Siri requests.

description: The app clearly handles missing configuration so users are told what to fix instead of seeing empty UI or unexplained request failures.
steps: Detect missing base URL or API key before attempting a request.
Present a clear on-screen message that points the operator to the settings screen.
Verify the same missing-config state can be surfaced as a Siri-spoken error.

description: Request execution state is visible in the app so operators can tell when the bridge is waiting on nanobot versus already failed locally.
steps: Add loading and in-flight state to the app-shell model.
Show request progress or disabled controls while a manual test is running.
Verify repeated manual sends do not produce ambiguous double-submit behavior.

description: The app shows the latest reply prominently so long answers remain inspectable even when Siri only speaks a truncated version.
steps: Keep the full display text of the latest backend reply visible in the app shell.
Ensure this latest-reply view updates after both success and failure states.
Verify long replies remain readable on-screen after spoken truncation is applied.

description: An `AskBridgeIntent` or equivalent App Intent exists so Siri has a dedicated, app-owned execution path instead of routing through brittle Shortcut prompts.
steps: Add the App Intent type inside the iOS subtree and wire it into the app target or intent extension as appropriate.
Make the intent depend on bridge-core request execution rather than reimplementing transport logic.
Verify the source is structured so Siri entrypoint code stays thin and declarative.

description: The Siri intent exposes a single free-text prompt parameter so users can ask nanobot arbitrary questions from iPhone Siri.
steps: Add a `prompt` string parameter to the App Intent.
Treat the prompt as the only required semantic input for v1.
Verify the parameter value flows unchanged into `BridgeRequest.prompt`.

description: The Siri intent defines `requestValueDialog` so missing-inline-input cases fall back to a spoken follow-up question instead of ending with `完成`.
steps: Configure the prompt parameter with a request dialog such as `你想问纳博特什么？`.
Keep the dialog text in a shared constant so branding changes remain localized.
Verify documentation and manual Siri tests expect the follow-up prompt path.

description: An `AppShortcutsProvider` registers the Siri phrases needed for both follow-up and inline-input invocation styles.
steps: Add an App Shortcuts provider or equivalent Siri phrase registration layer.
Register phrases in Chinese that map to the bridge intent without requiring the Shortcuts app.
Verify the provider references the same intent and prompt parameter used by the rest of the Siri path.

description: The no-inline-input phrase `问纳博特` is registered so Siri can launch the intent and ask a follow-up question when the prompt is omitted.
steps: Register the phrase `问纳博特` as a supported Siri invocation.
Ensure the no-inline phrase still reaches the same App Intent rather than a separate code path.
Verify the manual Siri test plan includes the follow-up-question flow.

description: The inline-input phrase `问纳博特 {prompt}` is registered so users can attempt a one-shot Siri invocation on iPhone without waiting for a follow-up prompt.
steps: Register an inline-prompt App Shortcut phrase for the intent.
Keep the inline phrase optional so the system can still fall back to follow-up collection if parsing fails.
Verify the Siri validation notes cover both inline success and follow-up fallback.

description: The Siri intent's `perform()` path executes the shared bridge runtime so Siri, manual app tests, and future ingress adapters all hit the same backend pipeline.
steps: Implement the intent `perform()` method to build a `BridgeRequest` and call the shared backend runtime.
Avoid direct URLSession or JSON handling inside the intent body.
Verify the perform path updates or records history in the same way as manual app requests where feasible.

description: Successful Siri requests return a spoken dialog derived from the backend reply so the user hears nanobot's answer instead of a generic Siri acknowledgement.
steps: Map successful `BridgeResponse` values into a Siri dialog result.
Prefer the computed spoken text over ad hoc local summarization.
Verify the success path never ends with a bare `完成` when a reply exists.

description: Failed Siri requests return a spoken, actionable error so missing config or network issues are debuggable from the voice experience itself.
steps: Map bridge errors into Siri dialog results with user-facing Chinese remediation hints.
Ensure missing config, timeout, auth, and network failures each surface distinct messages.
Verify the failure path does not silently succeed or collapse to a vague acknowledgement.

description: BridgeCore unit tests lock down nanobot request encoding and response decoding so backend contract regressions are caught before Siri testing.
steps: Add focused tests for `/chat` request JSON encoding and speaker selection.
Add focused tests for response decoding into `BridgeResponse`.
Verify the tests run without requiring a full iOS runtime or Siri environment.

description: BridgeCore unit tests lock down failure mapping and long-reply truncation so the spoken Siri experience stays deterministic and debuggable.
steps: Add tests for timeout, auth, offline, and malformed-JSON error mapping.
Add tests for spoken-text truncation versus full display text retention.
Verify the bridge returns stable user-facing text for each tested error case.

description: Manual in-app validation instructions exist so the operator can confirm `/chat` connectivity before involving Siri or App Intent behavior.
steps: Document how to enter `baseURL` and `apiKey` in the app.
Document a simple manual smoke prompt such as `你好` and the expected reply shape.
Verify the instructions reference only files or UI that actually exist in the subtree.

description: Manual Siri validation instructions exist for both follow-up and inline-input flows so iPhone Siri acceptance is repeatable after each harness round.
steps: Document the `嘿 Siri，问纳博特` follow-up flow and the expected spoken reply behavior.
Document the `嘿 Siri，问纳博特 你好` inline-input flow and the fallback expectations if inline parsing fails.
Verify the instructions distinguish iPhone Siri acceptance from non-goal HomePod testing.

description: Local development docs explain the current Xcode/toolchain requirement and the consequences of running with only Command Line Tools so future sessions do not misread build failures.
steps: Document the expected full-Xcode prerequisites for building the iOS app or running Siri/App Intent validation.
Record the current CommandLineTools-only state and its concrete symptoms if full Xcode is still absent.
Verify the docs point to the local environment gate rather than implying the code itself is broken.

description: Architecture docs reserve future ingress adapters for HomePod, 小爱同学, 天猫精灵, car head units, and other devices without pretending v1 already implements them.
steps: Document the ingress-adapter concept and list the reserved future platform surfaces.
Explain that v1 only implements iPhone Siri while the others remain protocol-level placeholders.
Verify the docs do not claim unimplemented platforms already work.

description: Architecture docs reserve future backend adapters for nanobot, openclaw, and related services so later expansion does not force a redesign of the bridge contract.
steps: Document nanobot as the only active backend and openclaw as the primary reserved future backend.
Explain how backend routing hangs off `BackendKind` without changing Siri or app-shell interfaces.
Verify the docs separate reserved architecture from actual v1 runtime support.

description: The completed Voice Bridge v1 work is revalidated against the repo baseline so introducing the iOS subtree does not break existing nanobot functionality or hide unresolved toolchain blockers.
steps: Re-run the relevant Swift, app-shell, and repo baseline checks after the Voice Bridge changes land.
Re-run `bash init.sh` and any focused bridge tests that are available in the current environment.
Record the final passing evidence and any remaining Xcode-only blockers in `PROGRESS.md` before cleanup or handoff.
